title: The reproductive toxicity and carcinogenicity of lead: A critical review
abstract:
AMERICAN JOURNAL OF INDUSTRIAL MEDICINE 38:231±243 (2000)
Review
The Reproductive Toxicity and
Carcinogenicity of Lead:
A Critical Review
Philip J. Landrigan, MD,1 Paolo Boffetta, MD,2 and Pietro Apostoli, MD3
INTRODUCTION
Lead is an ancient metal [Ramazzini, 1713]. Because of
its malleability, resistance to corrosion, and low melting
point, humans have used lead since prehistoric times to
fabricate statues, jewelry, water pipes, and drinking vessels.
The Romans used lead to sweeten wine [Eisinger, 1982].
Lead
forms
brilliantly
colored
salts,
and
lead-based
pigments have been used worldwide in cosmetics, ceramics,
and paint.
The quantity of lead used in the 20th century far
surpasses the total consumed in all previous eras. This heavy
recent use re¯ects industrial applications as well as the
consumption of vast quantities of lead as an anti-knock
agent in gasoline. In the United States alone, nearly 200,000
tons of lead were consumed annually as a gasoline additive
in the mid-1970s, the period of peak usage, and hundreds of
thousands of tons continue to be used worldwide [NAS,
1972]. Virtually all of the lead in gasoline is emitted into the
environment through vehicle exhaust in ®nely particulate
form. It causes contamination of air, dust, and soil, locally
and worldwide. Traces of automotive lead contamination
have been detected as far from centers of population as the
polar ice caps [Murozimi et al., 1969].
Lead is toxic to multiple organ systems, including the
central and peripheral nervous systems, the red blood cells,
the kidneys, the cardiovascular system, and the male and
female reproductive organs [Todd et al., 1996]. Lead is
especially toxic to the brain of the fetus and infant, and early
life exposure to lead at levels in blood as low as 10±20 mg/dl
causes loss of intelligence, shortening of attention span, and
disruption of behaviorÐeffects that appear to be irrever-
sible, untreatable, and lifelong in their consequences [CDC,
1991; NAS, 1993].
Lead is an established animal carcinogen. It is currently
considered by the International Agency for Research
on Cancer (IARC) to be a possible human carcinogen
(group 2B) on the basis of ``suf®cient'' animal data and
``inadequate'' human data [Silbergeld et al., 2000].
Recent research on the toxicology of lead has focused
on understanding health effects at relatively low levels of
exposure, levels that previously were considered safe
[Waldron and Stofen, 1974; Landrigan, 1989]. This work
has led to an understanding of ``subclinical toxicity,'' the
concept
that
relatively low-dose
exposure to certain
chemicals, lead among them, can cause harmful effects to
health that are not evident on the standard clinical
examination
[Waldron
and
Stofen,
1974;
Landrigan,
1989]. The underlying premise is that there exists a
continuum of toxicity, in which clinically apparent effects
have their asymptomatic, subclinical counterparts. Thus,
clinically obvious manifestations of lead poisoning such as
convulsions, wrist drop, anemia, and renal failure lie at the
upper end of the range of toxicity, while such covert effects

 as reduced intelligence, slowed nerve conduction, impaired
biosynthesis of heme, and altered excretion of uric acid are
their subclinical correlates. It is important to note that these
subclinical changes represent truly harmful events and are
not merely homeostatic or physiologic adjustments to the
presence of lead [Landrigan, 1989].
This review will examine new developments in under-
standing of the toxicity of lead. It will focus especially on
exposure assessment, reproductive toxicity, and carcino-
genicity. It considers new work on the molecular genetic
mechanisms of lead toxicity, and it examines recent
epidemiologic ®ndings. The review is based in large part
on research presented at an International Conference on
``Lead Exposure, Reproductive Toxicity and Carcinogeni-
city'' convened by the International Agency for Research on
Cancer and the University of Brescia, in collaboration with
the U.S. National Institute for Occupational Safety and
Health and the U.S National Institute of Environmental
Health Sciences on June 7±9, 1999 in Gargnano, Italy.
CHANGING PATTERNS OF LEAD
CONSUMPTION AND EXPOSURE;
NORTH±SOUTH DISPARITIES
Consumption of lead has decreased sharply in the
industrially developed nations in the past two decades. This
reduction re¯ects the phase-out of leaded gasoline as well as
decreases in industrial uses of lead [Hernberg, 2000]. Major
reductions in human exposure and in population blood lead
levels have resulted. In the USA, for instance, there occurred
a 90% decline in population blood lead levels between 1976
and 1997, [CDC, 1997]. This decline paralleled closely the
decline in use of leaded gasoline. At the 1999 Gargnano
Conference, data were presented documenting declines in
lead use, environmental contamination, and population
blood lead levels in industrially developed nations [Angerer
et al., 1999; Ganzi et al., 1999; Valkonen and Kallio, 1999].
In Germany, for example, Angerer et al. [1999] carried
out 48,346 blood lead analyses and 4,310 ALA determina-
tions in lead workers. About 20,000 of these investigations
were related to the ceramic and glass industry; 6,500 to
battery production; 2,300 to secondary lead smelters; and
the remaining 20,000 to other industrial areas. In 1989 the
median lead concentration in blood in this population was
24.2 mg/dl, while by 1998 it had fallen to 19.8 mg/dl. In 1989
the biological threshold limit (BAT) values valid in
Germany (700 mg/L for men and women not of childbearing
age, 300 mg/L for women of child-bearing age) were
exceeded in 1.9 and 18.6% of cases, respectively. By 1998
these percentages had fallen to 0.7 and 3.5%. Overall, it can
be assumed that occupational exposure to lead has
decreased. The current threshold limit values are most
frequently exceeded in battery production and in secondary
smelters.
Ganzi et al. [1999] presented the results of biological
monitoring in Italy since 1986 of about 1,400 women
occupationally exposed to lead (around 40% of all women
workers exposed to lead in the district); for about half of
them detailed information (age, working area and functions)
was available. About 600 of these women were in the fertile
age range (16±50 years). Variations in blood lead levels in
fertile women and in a subgroup of women with higher
probability of pregnancy (aged 20±38) have been examined.
A decrease in median values of both groups is evident, from
25 mg/dl in 1986 to 16 mg/dl in 1997 in the whole group of
fertile age and from 25 to 15 mg/dl in the subgroup with
higher probability for pregnancy. Examination of the
distribution of levels shows that, while in 1986 only 2% of
blood lead levels of fertile women were < 11 mg/dl, in 1997
this percentage rose to 27%. Likewise, in the group aged
10±38 years, the percentage was 3% in 1986, rising to 29%
in 1997.
Valkonen and Kallio [1999] considering 50,000 work-
ers from Finland constantly or occasionally exposed to lead,
demonstrated that occupational exposure to lead decreased
in the 1980s, and remained almost constant in the 1990s.
Median blood lead levels decreased during the period from
1973 to 1996 from 1.4 mmol/L to 0.4 mmol/L. In 1987 the
median value was 1.0 mmol/L and concentrations exceeding
the Finnish BAL were observed among 70 workers in 26
workplaces. Ten years later, the Finnish BAL was exceeded
among only 14 workers in 10 workplaces. No occupational
lead intoxications have been reported in Finland for many
years. In general, higher blood lead concentrations have
been observed among men than among women.
By contrast, in nations
undergoing transition
to
industrialization, lead use in gasoline as well as in industry
remains widespread, environmental contamination may be
intense, and blood lead levels in workers as well as in
residents of communities near polluting industries have
been reported to be dangerously elevated [Bindea, 1999;
Bulat et al., 1999; Chatterjee et al., 1999; Kaul et al., 1999;
Ikeda et al., 2000]. As examples, the contributions by Bulat
[1999] and Bindea [1999] may be cited. Bulat [1999]
described a signi®cant increase in incidence of lead
intoxication in lead smelter workers during 1996±98. All
cases were from one lead smelter and most had less than 1
year of exposure. The average blood lead levels were
3.10  0.81 mmol/L, the average erythrocyte protoporphyrin
levels 4.09  1.95 mmol/L, and the average urine d-
aminolaevulinic acid levels 411  92. All these patients
received chelation therapy (CaNa2EDTA). Bindea [1999]
examined lead levels in children from a polluted munici-
pality in Romania: Only six children out of 42 had blood
lead levels below 10 mg/dl, 16 had levels of 11±20 mg/dl,
nine had levels of 21±30 mg/dl, and 11 had levels above
30 mg/dl. A study carried out in July 1992 showed much
higher values.

The two populations at greatest risk of exposure to lead
in industrially developed as well as in developing nations
are workers and young children.
A wide variety of worker populations are at risk of
occupational exposure to lead. These include smelter
workers, battery makers, ceramics makers, ship burners
and construction workers. Virtually all occupational expo-
sure to lead is by inhalation; if airborne levels are controlled,
occupational exposure is controlled [Legge, 1934]. Most
nations have established industrial standards limiting air-
borne levels of lead in workplaces. Establishment and
enforcement of these standards has brought about well-
documented reductions in lead exposure and in blood lead
levels [Legge, 1934; Della Rosa et al., 1999; Angerer et al.,
1999; Alessio, 2000]. Unfortunately, there still remain many
thousands of workers worldwide with elevated blood lead
levels, and these persistent problems appear most severe in
developing nations. [Bulat et al., 1999]
Children absorb lead mainly by ingestion. They are at
high risk of exposure because of their normal oral explo-
ratory behavior coupled with the fact that typically they live
and play close to the ground [Lanphear et al., 1999]. Sources
of lead exposure for children include soil, dust, drinking
water, vinyl miniblinds, ceramics, lead-based paint, painted
glass, industrial emissions, [LeRoyer et al., 2000] lead trans-
ported home from the workplace, and occasionally toys.
Alternative medicines, which are growing in popularity
in developed countries, are occasionally reported as sources
of lead. Many people take these ``natural'' remedies in the
erroneous belief that they are always innocuous and can be
taken without medical supervision. Such remedies may,
however, be contaminated by heavy metals, such as lead,
and be a source of non-occupational exposure [Markowitz
et al., 1994; Muzi et al., 1999].
Routes of exposure to lead vary at different stages in
life [Lanphear et al., 1999]. The embryo and fetus are at risk
of transplacental exposure. Lead can be absorbed by the
mother either in the occupational setting [Landrigan et al.,
1990] or in the general environment, or it can be mobilized
from the maternal skeleton during pregnancy [Inskip et al.,
1999]. Once in the maternal bloodstream, lead moves
virtually without impediment across the placenta from
mother to fetus, and venous blood lead levels in mother and
infant have been shown to be nearly identical [Hertz-
During infancy, breast milk can be a source of lead
exposure. Lead mobilized from bony stores during lactation
is excreted in milk [Gulson et al., 1999a, 1999b].
In childhood, oral exploratory behavior, as noted above,
is the principal mechanism of exposure, and ingestion the
route [Lanphear et al., 1999].
In adult life, over 90% of lead poisoning is related to
occupation. Inhalation of airborne lead is the principal
means of exposure [Legge, 1934]. Airborne lead can exist
in either particulate or vapor form. Lead vapor is created
when lead is heated to a high temperature, as can occur in
lead smelting or in the burning or brazing of lead-painted
In old age, mobilization of lead from bones may be an
important source of exposure, particularly among persons
who have had past occupational exposure. Such mobiliza-
tion is thought to occur during the demineralization of the
skeleton that occurs in women at menopause and in men
throughout the aging process. In persons with substantial
bony stores of lead, the extent of this skeletal release can be
substantial. Such release may be associated with hyperten-
sion and other forms of cardiovascular disease [Silbergeld
et al., 1999] and also has been reported to be associated with
decreased cognition [Nordberg and Basun, 2000; Schwartz
ASSESSMENT OF EXPOSURE TO LEAD
Analytical advances over the past two decades have
greatly increased the sensitivity and precision of assays for
determination of levels of lead in the human body.
Several presentations have con®rmed that blood levels
in the general population in developed countries are in the
range 1±10 mg/dl, and in occupational groups are in the
range 20±40 mg/dl [Ikeda, 1999; Lanphear, 1999].
Measurement of recent exposure to lead is best
achieved by direct measurement of lead in whole blood.
The half-life of lead in blood is 36  5 days [Rabinowitz
et al., 1977]. Blood lead levels can be reliably measured at
concentrations as low as 1 mg/dl using atomic absorption
spectrophotometry. A most exciting recent advance is
the development of small, hand-held instrumentation for
rapid, direct measurement of lead in blood under ®eld
An interesting presentation by Salci et al. [1999] at the
Gargnano Conference described the development of a
simple and highly sensitive method for determination of
lead in plasma, whole blood, and urine by inductively
coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). This techni-
que permits examination of relationships among these three
indices of lead exposure. Plasma lead measurements by
ICP-MS showed good precision. The analytical recovery
was 105.9  4.3%, when 10 mg/L of lead was added to 20
plasma samples. The blood and urine measurements by ICP-
MS were nearly the same as those by AAS. In lead workers,
plasma lead levels (determined by ICP-MS) ranged from

0.25 to 17.18 mg/L, and were closely correlated with the
blood levels (range 30±724 mg/L; r  0.93). Urinary lead
levels (range 7.3±149.4 mg/g Cre) were also well correlated
with the blood levels (r  0.82). Plasma and urine levels
increased logarithmically with increasing blood level. A
linear relationship was found between plasma and urine lead
The use of stable lead isotope analysis has proven to
be an extremely sensitive methodology for tracing the
mobilization of lead from the skeleton as occurs, for
example, during pregnancy and lactation [Gulson et al.,
Measurement of cumulative past exposure to lead is
best achieved by direct, non-invasive measurement of lead
in bone by x-ray ¯uorescence (XRF) spectroscopy [Som-
ervaille et al., 1988; Todd et al., 1992]. Lead in the skeleton
is by far the largest component of the body lead burden,
[Rabinowitz et al., 1977] and it exists in two physiologically
distinct compartments. Lead in trabecular bone constitutes a
relatively mobile fraction of skeletal lead; by contrast, lead
in deep cortical bone is relatively immobile, and it is
calculated to have a half-life of approximately 27 years.
Lead concentration in cortical bone has been shown to
correlate closely with cumulative lead exposure [Somer-
vaille et al., 1988]. Lead in bone can be quanti®ed
accurately by XRF to levels as low as 10 mg/g lead of bone
Stewart et al. [1999] carried out a cross-sectional study
to identify predictors of tibial and dimercaptosuccinic acid
(DMSA)-chelatable lead levels in organolead manufactur-
ing workers with past exposure to organic and inorganic
lead. Tibial lead (measured by
¯uorescence), DMSA-chelatable lead (4-h urinary lead
excretion after oral administration 10 mg/kg), and several
exposure measures were obtained on 543 subjects with
mean age 57.6  7.6 (SD) years.
Tibial lead levels ranged from ÿ1.6 to 52.0 mg/g bone
mineral, with a mean (  SD) of 14.4  9.3 mg/g. DMSA-
chelatable lead levels ranged from 1.2 to 136 mg, with a
mean (  SD) of 19.3  17.2 mg. In a multiple linear
regression model of tibial lead levels, age (P < 0.01),
exposure duration (P < 0.01), current (P < 0.01) and past
(P  0.05) cigarette smoking, and diabetes (P  0.01) were
all independent positive predictors, while height (P  0.03)
and exercise inducing sweating (P  0.04) were negative
predictors. The ®nal regression model accounted for 31% of
the variance in tibial lead levels; 27% was explained by age
and exposure duration alone. DMSA-chelatable lead levels
(P < 0.01), and were negatively associated with diabetes
(P  0.02). The ®nal model accounted for 11% of the
variance in chelatable lead levels.
The low proportions of variance explained in these
models of both tibial and chelatable lead suggest that
additional factors are involved in the deposition of lead in
bone and soft tissue. In epidemiological studies of the health
effects of lead, evaluation of associations with both these
measures may allow inferences to be made about whether
health effects are likely to be recent, and thus potentially
reversible, or chronic, and thus possibly irreversible. The
data also provide direct evidence that in men the proportion
of total lead in the body that is bioavailable declines
A number of predictive models have been developed to
relate environmental lead levels to blood lead levels in
children and/or adults. These models include the U.S.
Environmental Protection Agency's (EPA's) Integrated
1994] the O'Flaherty Model, [O'Flaherty, 1998] the Leggett
Model, [Leggett, 1993] the U.S. EPA Adult Model
(modi®ed after Bowers et al., 1994), [Bowers et al., 1999]
and a model developed by Steenhout and coworkers [Beck
et al., 1999]. At least two of these models, the IEUBK and
the O'Flaherty Model, have been further modi®ed to include
a probabilistic exposure component to examine variability
and uncertainty in blood lead levels arising from speci®ed
Bowers et al. [1999] explored variability in permissible
exposure levels for both residential and occupational
settings. The IEUBK, ISE, O'Flaherty, and probabilistic
O'Flaherty models are used to address childhood exposures,
while the O'Flaherty and probabilistic O'Flaherty models
are used to address occupational or residential exposures to
adults. Variability in calculated permissible exposure levels
arises from the following factors: (1) differences in the
assumed values for exposure parameters de®ning lead
intake and absorption; (2) uncertainty in the pharmacoki-
netic parameters that translate lead uptake into blood lead
levels (such as the kinetic parameters controlling the rate of
bone lead deposition and remobilization); and (3) the
manner in which blood lead levels in the upper tail of the
distribution are estimated, e.g., whether by a probabilistic
analysis, or via a function of the single value produced by a
deterministic model such as the IEUBK model. Comparison
of these four models will allow examination of the effect of
different pharmacokinetic submodels (the IEUBK vs. the
O'Flaherty for children) and the effect of a probabilistic vs.
deterministic approach to exposure (the IEUBK vs. the ISE,
and the O'Flaherty vs. the probabilistic O'Flaherty) on the
calculation of permissible exposure levels. This comparison
(comparison of predictions with observations). In addition,
it emphasized the need for efforts to better de®ne the
exposure and pharmacokinetic parameters that are key
determinants of lead body burden.
Lead is a cumulative toxin that can produce multi-
system toxicity even at very low levels of exposure. Two
mechanisms by which it exerts its toxicity are (1) through its
af®nity with proteins and (2) its ability to mimic calcium
[Bernard, 1999; Silbergeld et al., 2000].
Interaction with proteins is an important mechanism
of lead toxicity, especially interactions with enzymes and
usually consists of reversible binding of lead to sulfhydryl
groups or to other protein sites capable of binding bivalent
cations (zinc, magnesium, calcium). A classical example is
the inhibition by lead of several key enzymes (e.g., d-
aminolaevulinic acid dehydratase) of the haem synthesis
pathway. Disruption of this critical pathway may result not
only in anaemia and decreased availability of cytochromes
for the respiratory chain, but also in the accumulation of
toxic metabolites such as d-aminolaevulinic acid (ALA).
The latter may undergo autoxidation, leading to generation
of free radicals which may cause lipid peroxidation and

Lead is an established animal carcinogen. It has been
shown that the kidney, speci®cally the tubular epithelium of
the renal cortex, is a major target organ in experimental
animals for the carcinogenicity of lead salts. Renal cortical
adenomas and carcinomas have been produced in rats and
mice of both sexes by systemic exposure to high doses of
various soluble salts of lead.
Evidence for a relation between lead and brain tumors
is less strong than that between lead and renal cancer, but
several studies have reported elevated incidences of brain
tumors in rats fed lead salts.
On the basis of these data, IARC has evaluated that
there exists ``suf®cient'' evidence for the carcinogenicity of
lead in animal species [IARC, 1987].

examined the evidence for both genotoxic and non-

concluded that lead is mostly negative in genotoxic assays,
although there is evidence that exposures of cells in culture
can induce chromosomal aberrations. Chromosomal aberra-

With respect to non-genotoxic mechanisms, Silbergeld
et al. found evidence that lead exposure in vitro inhibits
DNA repair. They noted that there is also some evidence that
lead has adverse effects on gap junctions between cells
[Mazzoleni et al., 1999]. Lead is also reported to cause
oxidative damage to DNA, but the evidence for that effect is
weak [Fu et al., 1999]. There are recent data that lead may
alter gene expression [Rossman, 2000]. One particularly
intriguing mechanism for alteration of gene expression is
that lead appears to interact with zinc-binding sites on
human protamine, an important DNA-associated protein
[Quintanilla-Vega et al., 2000]. Lead may also cause post-
translational changes in the tumor suppressor protein p53
[Quintanilla-Vega et al., 2000].

DNA damage caused by other exposures, rather than by

An important focus of current research on lead toxicity
is on improving delineation of dose±response relationships,
particularly at low levels of exposure. Intense interest
surrounds the development of biochemical and physiologic
markers of subclinical toxicity. Incorporation of such
markers into clinical and epidemiologic studies, in combi-
nation with newly developed markers of exposure assess-
ment, holds great promise for improving the understanding
of the toxicity of lead, particularly at low levels. Research is
also proceeding to examine further the in¯uence of genetic
polymorphisms on the mechanisms of toxicity and carcino-
In males, there is need to determine whether the toxic
effects of lead on reproductive function are demonstrable at
blood lead levels below 60 mg/dl. Such research will need to
examine not only testicular function, but also the possible
toxic effects of lead on the entire gonado-hypothalamic-
pituitary axis [Cullen et al., 1983]. Studies need to be
undertaken of the possible contribution of lead exposure to
reported downward temporal (secular) trends in sperm
In females, there is need to con®rm and extend the
interesting data presented at the Gargnano Conference
suggesting that even relatively low blood lead levels are
associated with decreased fertility and increased risk of
miscarriage [Borja-Aburto et al., 1999]. There is also need
to better delineate and quantify the mobilization of lead
from the skeleton that appears to occur in women at various
stages in the reproductive life-cycle, especially during
pregnancy, lactation, and menopause and to assess the
health effects that may result from that release. There is
need to further explore the interplay between genetic
The Toxicity and Carcinogenicity of Lead
polymorphisms and reproductive toxicity. There is need to
study further the possible relationship between lead and
``minor'' congenital malformations.
In future epidemiologic studies of the carcinogenicity
of lead, a major need exists to improve measurement of
cumulative exposure. The most promising approach at
present appears to be K-Xray ¯uorescence analysis of
lead in bone [Somervaille et al., 1988; Todd et al., 1992].
Such dose assessment may resolve apparent discrepancies
among studies and will permit a more accurate delinea-
tion of dose±response relationships. An argument may also
be made for longitudinal, prospective studies of lead
exposure that involve repeated measures of lead levels in
blood and bone. The exploitation of available cohorts of
workers with biomonitoring data, as it has been done in
Finland [Vasama-Neuvonen et al., 1999], represents an
ef®cient approach to assessing carcinogenicity of lead in
Another important need is to study in humans whether
lead is a transplacental carcinogen; in animal models, in
utero exposure to lead is a powerful risk factor for
development in early life of brain cancer [Silbergeld et al.,
2000]. Future research should also address the possible
differences in toxicity of different lead salts.

to lead in the workplace and in the general environment is
required in nations around the world. Strong, scienti®-
cally based regulations are required, and they must be

developing nations are not ethically defensible.

Lead is a known reproductive toxin. In males, it causes
reduction in sperm count and reductions in fertility. In
females, it increases risk of miscarriage. Recent research has
shown that these reproductive effects occur at relatively low
levels of exposure that previously were considered safe.
Research to assess these effects at ever lower levels of
exposure is ongoing and much needed.
Lead is a potent neurodevelopmental toxin. Prospective
longitudinal studies of outstanding quality demonstrate that
prenatal and early postnatal exposure to lead at levels as low
as 10±20 mg/dl results in damage to the central nervous
system [Bellinger et al., 1987; Wasserman et al, 1997]. This
damage results in diminished intelligence and altered
behavior [CDC, 1991; NAS, 1993]. These effects appear
to be irreversible, untreatable, and lifelong [Needleman
et al., 1990]. The only rational approach to their control is to
reduce prenatal and early life exposure to lead.
Lead is a proven, ``suf®cient'' animal carcinogen. It can
cause renal cancer and possibly brain tumors in rats and
mice. In humans, IARC had previously considered the
evidence ``inadequate'' to assess the carcinogenicity of lead.
However, new data have accumulated on the cancer risk of
workers exposed to lead, including results presented at
the 1999 Conference in Gargnano. Such new data would
justify a re-evaluation of the available evidence in the near
